title: Def Poets Society

jokes.text: Look, Geoffrey. We're voguing. And what is the precise nature of this nervous disorder?
jokes.setup: Will excitedly shows Geoffrey the voguing dance trend
jokes.punchline: Geoffrey's dry, clinical response treating dancing as a medical condition
jokes.explanation: Perfect Geoffrey character moment - his stuffy British butler personality clashing with 90s dance culture. Well-structured contrast.

jokes.text: It's for people that want to look cool but ain't really got no rhythm.
jokes.setup: Geoffrey asks what voguing is
jokes.punchline: Will's brutally honest assessment that it's for unrhythmic people trying to look cool
jokes.explanation: Solid observational humor about the dance trend, delivered in Will's characteristic blunt style.

jokes.text: You should be good at it. Come on, strike the pose. Unhand me, you ruffian.
jokes.setup: Will tells Geoffrey he should be good at voguing since it's for people without rhythm
jokes.punchline: Geoffrey's indignant Victorian response to being physically handled
jokes.explanation: Great character integration - 'ruffian' is perfectly Geoffrey, and the physical comedy of forced dancing works well.

jokes.text: Yeah, but Carlton ain't home yet.
jokes.setup: Aunt Vivian asks if Will has anything better to do than annoy Geoffrey
jokes.punchline: Will admits his primary entertainment is annoying his cousin Carlton
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy and character moment - establishes Will's relationship with Carlton in just six words.

jokes.text: Window-shop at Randy's Short and Small?
jokes.setup: Vivian asks what Carlton does after school
jokes.punchline: Will suggests Carlton shops at a store specifically for short people
jokes.explanation: Brilliant fictional store name that's both specific and character-driven. The alliteration makes it highly quotable.

jokes.text: I belong to the home shopping club.
jokes.setup: Discussion about Carlton belonging to after-school clubs
jokes.punchline: Will twists the concept to reference TV home shopping, not an actual club
jokes.explanation: Decent wordplay on 'club' but somewhat predictable. Shows Will's lazy attitude toward activities.

jokes.text: And you won't get into any of the good nightclubs. I'll never meet Lisa Bonet.
jokes.setup: Uncle Phil warns about becoming shallow without outside interests
jokes.punchline: Will escalates the consequences to not getting into nightclubs and missing celebrities
jokes.explanation: Period-appropriate celebrity reference (Lisa Bonet was huge in 1990) but relies on name-dropping rather than wit.

jokes.text: Bring on the clubs.
jokes.setup: Vivian threatens Will with getting an after-school job
jokes.punchline: Will's immediate reversal when faced with actual work
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective character moment showing Will's priorities and quick thinking under pressure.

jokes.text: If I had the club of my choice, you'd be lying on the floor unconscious.
jokes.setup: Will tells Carlton to find the club of his choice
jokes.punchline: Carlton's unexpectedly violent response about wanting a weapon
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly subverts expectations - clean-cut Carlton making a violent threat. Great character integration showing his frustration with Will.

jokes.text: Lead me to the sisters. I'm ready to get extracurricular.
jokes.setup: Learning the clubs include girls from sister schools
jokes.punchline: Will plays on 'sisters' and 'extracurricular' with sexual innuendo
jokes.explanation: Solid wordplay that fits Will's character motivation perfectly - he's only interested because of girls.

jokes.text: Call me Ned. You need another member of this poetry club? Will, solid. We could really use a representative of the Black male urban experience.
jokes.setup: White teacher trying to be cool and connect with Will
jokes.punchline: Awkwardly reducing Will to a demographic checkbox
jokes.explanation: Good cringe comedy showing well-meaning but clueless white teacher tokenizing Will. Feels authentic to the character.

jokes.text: More specifically, the handsome Black male urban experience.
jokes.setup: Teacher's awkward categorization of Will
jokes.punchline: Will sarcastically adding 'handsome' to mock the reductive labeling
jokes.explanation: Smart callback that shows Will's awareness of being tokenized while also showing his confidence. Good character moment.

jokes.text: If she didn't, her daddy would have.
jokes.setup: Learning Sylvia Plath killed herself after writing a poem about her father
jokes.punchline: Will suggests the father would have killed her for the harsh poem
jokes.explanation: Dark but clever observation about the harsh content of Plath's poem. Excellent economy - packs a big laugh into few words.

jokes.text: Raphael de la Ghetto.
jokes.setup: Pressured to name the poet who wrote his improvised poem
jokes.punchline: Made-up name combining Renaissance artist Raphael with street slang 'de la Ghetto'
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly constructed fake name that's both sophisticated and street. Becomes episode's main running gag. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: I am a poet and I do know it. Why don't you recite your poem?
jokes.setup: Will says 'Floor's yours. You're a poet and you don't know it'
jokes.punchline: Christina corrects the common phrase, then immediately calls his bluff
jokes.explanation: Simple wordplay correction followed by putting Will on the spot. Functional but not particularly clever.

jokes.text: No, baby, he used to. He went to the clinic. He's straight now.
jokes.setup: Girl says de la Ghetto uses a lot of metaphor in his poetry
jokes.punchline: Will misunderstands 'metaphor' as 'methadone' and references drug treatment
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection joke showing Will's street knowledge vs academic vocabulary gap. Perfect character moment with great structure.

jokes.text: Poem? Then what you going to do, bake some cookies? Hem a dress?
jokes.setup: Jazz discovers Will is writing poetry
jokes.punchline: Jazz mocks poetry as feminine with stereotypical domestic activities
jokes.explanation: Dated gender stereotypes that fit Jazz's character but lack originality. Functional setup for Will's defense.

jokes.text: Let's go get some barbecue and get busy.
jokes.setup: Will needs help finishing a romantic poem
jokes.punchline: Jazz offers the least romantic line possible as the romantic climax
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jazz character moment - completely missing the point of romance. The juxtaposition with flowery poetry makes it brilliant.

jokes.text: Roses are red Violets are blue Pansies are poets Will, tell me it isn't true
jokes.setup: Carlton mocking Will for writing poetry
jokes.punchline: Twisting the classic poem format to insult poets as 'pansies'
jokes.explanation: Solid parody of the roses are red format. Fits Carlton's preppy character and sets up Will's comeback.

jokes.text: Roses are red Violets are blue Jazz and I are Black, But Carlton, what are you?
jokes.setup: Carlton's roses are red poem mocking Will
jokes.punchline: Will's comeback questioning Carlton's racial/cultural identity
jokes.explanation: Perfect comeback that hits at the show's central theme - Carlton's disconnect from Black culture. Excellent character integration.

jokes.text: The crowd rose to its feet shouting, 'Encore!'
jokes.setup: Geoffrey claims he won first prize at a 1963 poetry recital
jokes.punchline: His proud memory of the crowd's enthusiastic response
jokes.explanation: Character moment showing Geoffrey's past glory days and cultural pretensions. Sets up the later poetry reveal.

jokes.text: Looks like you eat here often.
jokes.setup: Uncle Phil throws Jazz out and says he eats here never
jokes.punchline: Jazz's comeback about Phil's weight before getting thrown out
jokes.explanation: Standard fat joke but fits the Jazz-Phil dynamic. The timing of getting the insult in before being ejected works.

jokes.text: Because girls think with their brains.
jokes.setup: Will asks why girls like poetry if guys only do it to get girls
jokes.punchline: Vivian's pointed response about gender differences in motivation
jokes.explanation: Sharp comeback that puts Will in his place while making a point about gender stereotypes. Great economy.

jokes.text: Will and Christina sitting in a tree... Beat it.
jokes.setup: Ashley starts the classic children's teasing rhyme
jokes.punchline: Will's immediate shutdown before she can finish
jokes.explanation: Simple sibling dynamic humor. Ashley's teasing and Will's embarrassed reaction feel authentic.

jokes.text: Wears the same old dashiki and monster afro.
jokes.setup: Will describes the fictional poet Raphael de la Ghetto
jokes.punchline: Creating an exaggerated stereotype of 1960s Black radical appearance
jokes.explanation: Builds the mythology of the fake poet while playing with stereotypes. Important setup for later visual gags.

jokes.text: Wears the same old dashiki and monster afro.
jokes.setup: Describing the fake Raphael de la Ghetto
jokes.punchline: Will's specific 1960s stereotype description
jokes.explanation: Builds the absurd fake poet character with specific visual details that will pay off later

jokes.text: You wouldn't either if you had the same dashiki on for 20 years.
jokes.setup: Will says Raphael doesn't like to leave the house
jokes.punchline: Callback to the unchanging wardrobe, adding hygiene concerns
jokes.explanation: Good callback that deepens the fake poet's mythology while adding gross-out humor.

jokes.text: I'm tired of those grass burns.
jokes.setup: Jazz recognizes Uncle Phil and considers leaving
jokes.punchline: Reference to repeatedly being thrown out onto the lawn
jokes.explanation: Good callback to the show's running physical gag, adding the detail of actual injury from repeated tosses.

jokes.text: He has to be able to read? That'll cost you two extra Jacksons, but I know a guy.
jokes.setup: Will asks if the fake poet can read
jokes.punchline: Jazz treats literacy as an expensive add-on service
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jazz character moment - his street connections are so sketchy that literacy is premium. Great absurdist logic.

jokes.text: Here's an extra $10. Get me a Rolex.
jokes.setup: Discussion of paying for services
jokes.punchline: Carlton casually asks for a luxury watch for ten dollars
jokes.explanation: Shows Carlton's sheltered nature - he doesn't understand the value of money or that these are fake goods.

jokes.text: Will's gonna get thanked!
jokes.setup: Christina hints at thanking Will after the poetry reading
jokes.punchline: Jazz's excited interpretation of what 'thanked' means
jokes.explanation: Jazz's juvenile excitement about Will's romantic prospects. Fits his character's immaturity.

jokes.text: We heard the buzz about this event on Melrose.
jokes.setup: Explaining why they came to poetry night
jokes.punchline: Claiming a high school poetry reading is trendy enough for Melrose Avenue buzz
jokes.explanation: Excellent Hilary absurdism - treating high school event like LA cultural scene perfectly captures her character

jokes.text: That's right. I suppose I just get my horoscope out of the air.
jokes.setup: Will says he's never seen Hilary read anything
jokes.punchline: Hilary's sarcastic response about how she gets information
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hilary moment - airheaded but with surprising wit when defending herself. Good character integration.

jokes.text: Locked up in the steel hotel. Cops caught him with a fistful of toy Rolexes.
jokes.setup: Will asks where the fake poet is
jokes.punchline: Jazz explains the arrest using slang for jail and referencing fake watches
jokes.explanation: Great street slang ('steel hotel') and callbacks to the fake merchandise subplot. Pure Jazz character voice.

jokes.text: This crowd will get ugly if they got to listen to much more of that.
jokes.setup: Bad poetry being recited at the event
jokes.punchline: Jazz's assessment that the audience is getting hostile
jokes.explanation: Good observation about bad poetry performance. Jazz as the voice of the audience's patience wearing thin.

jokes.text: There was extenuating circumstances.
jokes.setup: Will complains the fake poet didn't show up
jokes.punchline: Jazz's incorrect grammar while trying to sound sophisticated
jokes.explanation: Classic Jazz character moment - trying to sound smart but getting basic grammar wrong. Shows his limited education.

jokes.text: Listen, or I'll kill you
jokes.setup: Geoffrey in disguise reciting street poetry
jokes.punchline: Ending a poem about listening with a death threat
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist moment - posh Geoffrey delivering street threats. The contrast between his refined nature and the violent content is perfect.

jokes.text: Yeah. It's a good look for him.
jokes.setup: Realizing it's Geoffrey in disguise
jokes.punchline: Hilary's shallow focus on the fashion aspect
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hilary response - completely missing the situation's absurdity to focus on superficial appearance

jokes.text: Silence, you ungrateful children. When you're ready to be quiet and listen, I shall return.
jokes.setup: Audience is talking during the poetry performance
jokes.punchline: Geoffrey's aristocratic scolding breaks character completely
jokes.explanation: Perfect character break moment - Geoffrey's refined nature completely overwhelms his disguise. Excellent character integration.

jokes.text: I worked all that out in therapy.
jokes.setup: Christina asks where his afro and dashiki are
jokes.punchline: Jazz explains the outdated look was a psychological issue he resolved
jokes.explanation: Clever modern explanation for outdated stereotypes. Good misdirection that makes the fake poet more believable.

jokes.text: I worked all that out in therapy.
jokes.setup: Christina asking about missing afro and dashiki
jokes.punchline: Jazz's casual reference to therapy solving his 1960s street poet appearance
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist logic - treating fashion choices as psychological issues resolved through therapy is perfectly unexpected

jokes.text: Let's go get some barbecue And get busy
jokes.setup: Jazz recites Will's romantic poem to impress Christina
jokes.punchline: Using Jazz's earlier terrible romantic line as the poem's climax
jokes.explanation: Brilliant payoff to earlier setup. The terrible line actually works in context, and Christina's positive reaction makes it even funnier. Perfect structure and callback.

jokes.text: Let's go get some barbecue And get busy
jokes.setup: Jazz reciting Will's poem to prove his identity
jokes.punchline: Including his terrible suggested final line
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback payoff - Jazz's terrible line from earlier now works as 'proof' of his poet identity, completing the absurd circle

jokes.text: Tell me more.
jokes.setup: Jazz delivers the worst possible romantic line
jokes.punchline: Christina is actually impressed and wants to hear more
jokes.explanation: Perfect reaction beat that subverts expectations. The fact that the terrible line works makes the comedy even better.

jokes.text: Works every time.
jokes.setup: Christina responds positively to his terrible pickup line
jokes.punchline: Jazz's smug confirmation that his method is foolproof
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment showing Jazz was right all along. Great economy and callback structure.

jokes.text: I'm a poet, and it's a lot more ink in this pen.
jokes.setup: Will lost Christina but reflects on the experience
jokes.punchline: Will uses writing metaphor to say he has more romantic opportunities
jokes.explanation: Decent metaphor that shows Will's resilience and way with words. Good character recovery moment.

jokes.text: Psych. We just kidding. Good night, y'all.
jokes.setup: Vivian starts reading serious poetry as the episode winds down
jokes.punchline: Will breaks the fourth wall to end the fake-serious moment
jokes.explanation: Classic Fresh Prince fourth-wall break. Good way to end the episode by acknowledging they were putting on a serious act.

tmdb_overview: Will joins his prep school's poetry club.
